en.extract:
The Geelvink cicadabird (Edolisoma meyerii) is a passerine bird in the family Campephagidae that is found on the islands of Numfor and Biak in the Geelvink Bay of New Guinea. It was formerly considered to be conspecific with the common cicadabird, now renamed the Sahul cicadabird.


Taxonomy
The Geelvink cicadbird was formally described in 1878 by the Italian zoologist Tommaso Salvadori based on a specimen collected on the island of Biak in the Geelvink Bay of New Guinea. He coined the binomial name Edolisoma meyerii where the specific epithet was chosen to honour the German physician Adolf Bernhard Meyer. The Geelvink cicadbird was formerly treated as conspecific with the common cicadabird (now renamed the Sahul cicadabird) (Edolisoma tenuirostre) but has been elevated to species status based on plumage differences and a molecular phylogenetic study published in 2018.
Two subspecies are recognised:

E. m. numforanum (Peters, JL & Mayr, 1960) – Numfor (Geelvink Bay Islands, northwest New Guinea)
E. m. meyerii Salvadori, 1878 – Biak (Geelvink Bay Islands, northwest New Guinea)


References

The '''Geelvink cicadabird''' ('''''Edolisoma meyerii''''') is a [[passerine]] bird in the family [[Campephagidae]] that is found on the islands of [[Numfor]] and [[Biak]] in the [[Cenderawasih Bay|Geelvink Bay]] of New Guinea. It was formerly considered to be [[conspecific]] with the common cicadabird, now renamed the [[Sahul cicadabird]].

==Taxonomy==
The Geelvink cicadbird was [[species description|formally described]] in 1878 by the Italian zoologist [[Tommaso Salvadori]] based on a specimen collected on the island of [[Biak]] in the [[Cenderawasih Bay|Geelvink Bay]] of New Guinea. He coined the [[binomial name]] ''Edolisoma meyerii'' where the specific epithet was chosen to honour the German physician [[Adolf Bernhard Meyer]].<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Salvadori | first=Tommaso | author-link=Tommaso Salvadori | year=1878 | title=Descrizione di trentuna specie nuove di uccelli della sottoregione papuana, e note intorno ad altre poco conosciute | language=Latin, Italian | journal=Annali del Museo Civico di Storia Naturale di Genova | volume=12 | pages=317–347 [327–328] | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/30263901 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor1-last=Mayr | editor1-first=Ernst | editor1-link=Ernst Mayr | editor2-last=Greenway | editor2-first=James C. Jr | year=1960 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=9 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology  | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=187 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14480862 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite web | last=Jobling | first=James A. | title=meyerii | work=The Key to Scientific Names | url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/key-to-scientific-names/search?q=meyerii | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | access-date=23 September 2024 }}</ref> The Geelvink cicadbird was formerly treated as [[conspecific]] with the common cicadabird (now renamed the [[Sahul cicadabird]]) (''Edolisoma tenuirostre'') but has been elevated to species status based on plumage differences and a [[molecular phylogenetic]] study published in 2018.<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | date=August 2024 | title=Bristlehead, butcherbirds, woodswallows, Mottled Berryhunter, ioras, cuckooshrikes | work=IOC World Bird List Version 14.2 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/butcherbirds/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=23 September 2024 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal | last1=Pedersen | first1=M.P. | last2=Irestedt | first2=M. | last3=Joseph | first3=L. | last4=Rahbek | first4=C. | last5=Jønsson | first5=K.A. | date=2018 | title=Phylogeography of a 'great speciator' (Aves: ''Edolisoma tenuirostre'') reveals complex dispersal and diversification dynamics across the Indo-Pacific | journal=Journal of Biogeography | volume=45 | issue=4 | pages=826–837 | doi=10.1111/jbi.13182| bibcode=2018JBiog..45..826P | hdl=11250/2593769 | hdl-access=free }}</ref>

Two subspecies are recognised:<ref name=ioc/>
* ''E. m. numforanum'' ([[James L. Peters|Peters, JL]] & [[Ernst Mayr|Mayr]], 1960) – [[Numfor]] ([[Cenderawasih Bay|Geelvink Bay Islands]], northwest [[New Guinea]])
* ''E. m. meyerii'' [[Tommaso Salvadori|Salvadori]], 1878 – [[Biak]] ([[Cenderawasih Bay|Geelvink Bay]] Islands, northwest [[New Guinea]])
